@graph.headline: Unlocking Node.js Performance: Harnessing Multi-Core Power with the cluster Module
@graph.description: Learn how to effectively utilize all CPU cores in your Node.js applications using the built-in cluster module, improve performance and resilience, and understand when this approach is preferable to other scaling solutions.
@graph.keywords: Node.js, cluster module, multi-core, worker_threads, performance, scaling, IPC, PM2, Docker, Kubernetes, Graceful Shutdown

hasPart.name: Simple Node.js Clustering Example
hasPart.description: A basic clustered HTTP server utilizing the node:cluster, node:http, node:os, and node:process modules.

mainEntity.name: Why is my Node.js application only using a single CPU core?

acceptedAnswer.text: By default, a single Node.js process runs on a single CPU core. If you have a multi-core server without utilizing tools like the built-in cluster module, your other cores remain idle, capping your performance to that single core.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the Node.js cluster module and worker_threads?

acceptedAnswer.text: The cluster module scales I/O-bound workloads by creating separate processes, each with its own memory space. worker_threads offload CPU-intensive tasks to separate threads within the same process, sharing memory space without blocking the main event loop.

mainEntity.name: Why should I use the cluster module instead of PM2, Docker, or Kubernetes?

acceptedAnswer.text: While Docker and Kubernetes are for orchestration at scale, the cluster module is a simpler, dependency-free solution for utilizing multiple CPU cores for a single app on a single machine. PM2 actually uses the cluster module under the hood.

mainEntity.name: When is the cluster module the right choice for my application?

acceptedAnswer.text: It is the ideal choice for I/O-bound applications like web servers, improving throughput on multi-core machines, and adding basic fault tolerance by restarting crashed workers.

mainEntity.name: How does graceful shutdown work with the cluster module?

acceptedAnswer.text: Using Inter-Process Communication (IPC), the primary process can catch shutdown signals and instruct workers to stop accepting new connections and finish existing requests before exiting, preventing abrupt disconnections.
